# determine dimensional coordinate names and a dict mapping name to DataArray
def _parse_datasets(datasets):

    dims = set()
    all_coord_names = set()
    data_vars = set()  # list of data_vars
    dim_coords = {}  # maps dim name to variable
    dims_sizes = {}  # shared dimension sizes to expand variables

    for ds in datasets:
        dims_sizes.update(ds.dims)
        all_coord_names.update(ds.coords)
        data_vars.update(ds.data_vars)

        for dim in set(ds.dims) - dims:
            if dim not in dim_coords:
                dim_coords[dim] = ds.coords[dim].variable
        dims = dims | set(ds.dims)

    return dim_coords, dims_sizes, all_coord_names, data_vars

    """
    from .dataset import Dataset

    dim, coord = _calc_concat_dim_coord(dim)
    # Make sure we're working on a copy (we'll be loading variables)
    datasets = [ds.copy() for ds in datasets]
    datasets = align(
        *datasets, join=join, copy=False, exclude=[dim], fill_value=fill_value
    )

    dim_coords, dims_sizes, coord_names, data_names = _parse_datasets(datasets)
    dim_names = set(dim_coords)
    unlabeled_dims = dim_names - coord_names

    both_data_and_coords = coord_names & data_names
    if both_data_and_coords:
        raise ValueError(
            "%r is a coordinate in some datasets but not others." % both_data_and_coords
        )
    # we don't want the concat dimension in the result dataset yet
    dim_coords.pop(dim, None)
    dims_sizes.pop(dim, None)

def test_concat_do_not_promote(self):
        # GH438
        objs = [
            Dataset({"y": ("t", [1])}, {"x": 1, "t": [0]}),
            Dataset({"y": ("t", [2])}, {"x": 1, "t": [0]}),
        ]
        expected = Dataset({"y": ("t", [1, 2])}, {"x": 1, "t": [0, 0]})
        actual = concat(objs, "t")
        assert_identical(expected, actual)

        objs = [
            Dataset({"y": ("t", [1])}, {"x": 1, "t": [0]}),
            Dataset({"y": ("t", [2])}, {"x": 2, "t": [0]}),
        ]
        with pytest.raises(ValueError):
            concat(objs, "t", coords="minimal")

    def test_concat_dim_is_variable(self):
        objs = [Dataset({"x": 0}), Dataset({"x": 1})]
        coord = Variable("y", [3, 4])
        expected = Dataset({"x": ("y", [0, 1]), "y": [3, 4]})
        actual = concat(objs, coord)
        assert_identical(actual, expected)

    def test_concat_multiindex(self):
        x = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([[1, 2, 3], ["a", "b"]])
        expected = Dataset({"x": x})
        actual = concat(
            [expected.isel(x=slice(2)), expected.isel(x=slice(2, None))], "x"
        )
        assert expected.equals(actual)
        assert isinstance(actual.x.to_index(), pd.MultiIndex)

    @pytest.mark.parametrize("fill_value", [dtypes.NA, 2, 2.0])
    def test_concat_fill_value(self, fill_value):
        datasets = [
            Dataset({"a": ("x", [2, 3]), "x": [1, 2]}),
            Dataset({"a": ("x", [1, 2]), "x": [0, 1]}),
        ]
        if fill_value == dtypes.NA:
            # if we supply the default, we expect the missing value for a
            # float array
            fill_value = np.nan
        expected = Dataset(
            {"a": (("t", "x"), [[fill_value, 2, 3], [1, 2, fill_value]])},
            {"x": [0, 1, 2]},
        )
        actual = concat(datasets, dim="t", fill_value=fill_value)
        assert_identical(actual, expected)

def concat(
    objs,
    dim,
    data_vars="all",
    coords="different",
    compat="equals",
    positions=None,
    fill_value=dtypes.NA,
    join="outer",
):
    """Concatenate xarray objects along a new or existing dimension.

    Parameters
    ----------
    objs : sequence of Dataset and DataArray objects
        xarray objects to concatenate together. Each object is expected to
        consist of variables and coordinates with matching shapes except for
        along the concatenated dimension.
    dim : str or DataArray or pandas.Index
        Name of the dimension to concatenate along. This can either be a new
        dimension name, in which case it is added along axis=0, or an existing
        dimension name, in which case the location of the dimension is
        unchanged. If dimension is provided as a DataArray or Index, its name
        is used as the dimension to concatenate along and the values are added
        as a coordinate.
    data_vars : {'minimal', 'different', 'all' or list of str}, optional
        These data variables will be concatenated together:
          * 'minimal': Only data variables in which the dimension already
            appears are included.
          * 'different': Data variables which are not equal (ignoring
            attributes) across all datasets are also concatenated (as well as
            all for which dimension already appears). Beware: this option may
            load the data payload of data variables into memory if they are not
            already loaded.
          * 'all': All data variables will be concatenated.
          * list of str: The listed data variables will be concatenated, in
            addition to the 'minimal' data variables.
        If objects are DataArrays, data_vars must be 'all'.
    coords : {'minimal', 'different', 'all' or list of str}, optional
        These coordinate variables will be concatenated together:
          * 'minimal': Only coordinates in which the dimension already appears
            are included.
          * 'different': Coordinates which are not equal (ignoring attributes)
            across all datasets are also concatenated (as well as all for which
            dimension already appears). Beware: this option may load the data
            payload of coordinate variables into memory if they are not already
            loaded.
          * 'all': All coordinate variables will be concatenated, except
            those corresponding to other dimensions.
          * list of str: The listed coordinate variables will be concatenated,
            in addition to the 'minimal' coordinates.
    compat : {'identical', 'equals', 'broadcast_equals', 'no_conflicts', 'override'}, optional
        String indicating how to compare non-concatenated variables of the same name for
        potential conflicts. This is passed down to merge.

        - 'broadcast_equals': all values must be equal when variables are
          broadcast against each other to ensure common dimensions.
        - 'equals': all values and dimensions must be the same.
        - 'identical': all values, dimensions and attributes must be the
          same.
        - 'no_conflicts': only values which are not null in both datasets
          must be equal. The returned dataset then contains the combination
          of all non-null values.
        - 'override': skip comparing and pick variable from first dataset
    positions : None or list of integer arrays, optional
        List of integer arrays which specifies the integer positions to which
        to assign each dataset along the concatenated dimension. If not
        supplied, objects are concatenated in the provided order.
    fill_value : scalar, optional
        Value to use for newly missing values
    join : {'outer', 'inner', 'left', 'right', 'exact'}, optional
        String indicating how to combine differing indexes
        (excluding dim) in objects

        - 'outer': use the union of object indexes
        - 'inner': use the intersection of object indexes
        - 'left': use indexes from the first object with each dimension
        - 'right': use indexes from the last object with each dimension
        - 'exact': instead of aligning, raise `ValueError` when indexes to be
          aligned are not equal
        - 'override': if indexes are of same size, rewrite indexes to be
          those of the first object with that dimension. Indexes for the same
          dimension must have the same size in all objects.

    indexers, mode, concat_over : deprecated

    Returns
    -------
    concatenated : type of objs

    See also
    --------
    merge
    auto_combine
    """
    # TODO: add ignore_index arguments copied from pandas.concat
    # TODO: support concatenating scalar coordinates even if the concatenated
    # dimension already exists
    from .dataset import Dataset
    from .dataarray import DataArray

    try:
        first_obj, objs = utils.peek_at(objs)
    except StopIteration:
        raise ValueError("must supply at least one object to concatenate")

    if compat not in _VALID_COMPAT:
        raise ValueError(
            "compat=%r invalid: must be 'broadcast_equals', 'equals', 'identical', 'no_conflicts' or 'override'"
            % compat
        )

    if isinstance(first_obj, DataArray):

            "can only concatenate xarray Dataset and DataArray "
            "objects, got %s" % type(first_obj)

    return f(objs, dim, data_vars, coords, compat, positions, fill_value, join)

def broadcast_dimension_size(variables: List[Variable],) -> Dict[Hashable, int]:
    """Extract dimension sizes from a dictionary of variables.

    Raises ValueError if any dimensions have different sizes.
    """
    dims: Dict[Hashable, int] = {}
    for var in variables:
        for dim, size in zip(var.dims, var.shape):
            if dim in dims and size != dims[dim]:
                raise ValueError("index %r not aligned" % dim)
            dims[dim] = size
    return dims

        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        dims : str or sequence of str
            Name(s) of the the data dimension(s). Must be either a string (only
            for 1D data) or a sequence of strings with length equal to the
            number of dimensions.
        data : array_like
            Data array which supports numpy-like data access.
        attrs : dict_like or None, optional
            Attributes to assign to the new variable. If None (default), an
            empty attribute dictionary is initialized.
        encoding : dict_like or None, optional
            Dictionary specifying how to encode this array's data into a
            serialized format like netCDF4. Currently used keys (for netCDF)
            include '_FillValue', 'scale_factor', 'add_offset' and 'dtype'.
            Well-behaved code to serialize a Variable should ignore
            unrecognized encoding items.
        """
        self._data = as_compatible_data(data, fastpath=fastpath)
        self._dims = self._parse_dimensions(dims)
        self._attrs = None
        self._encoding = None
        if attrs is not None:
            self.attrs = attrs
        if encoding is not None:
            self.encoding = encoding

    @property
    def dtype(self):
        return self._data.dtype

    @property
    def shape(self):
        return self._data.shape

    @property
    def nbytes(self):
        return self.size * self.dtype.itemsize

    def _in_memory(self):
        return isinstance(self._data, (np.ndarray, np.number, PandasIndexAdapter)) or (

        )

## Root Cause Analysis

The bug is in `xarray/core/concat.py` in the `_dataset_concat` function. When concatenating datasets with different variables, the code explicitly raises errors in two places:

1. **Lines 315-320**: When processing "variables to merge" (coordinates/data_vars that are equal across datasets):
```python
absent_merge_vars = variables_to_merge - set(ds.variables)
if absent_merge_vars:
    raise ValueError(
        "variables %r are present in some datasets but not others. "
        % absent_merge_vars
    )
```

2. **Lines 359-364**: When iterating over variables from the first dataset:
```python
for k in datasets[0].variables:
    if k in concat_over:
        try:
            vars = ensure_common_dims([ds.variables[k] for ds in datasets])
        except KeyError:
            raise ValueError("%r is not present in all datasets." % k)
```

The code already has infrastructure for handling missing values via the `fill_value` parameter - it's passed to `align()` but only used for coordinates/index alignment, not for data variables that are missing from some datasets.

## Locations That Need to Change

Here's the complete fix addressing each location:

### 1. Variables to Merge (lines 311-331)
**Current behavior**: Raises an error when variables are present in some datasets but not others
**Fix**: Create placeholder variables filled with `fill_value` for missing variables

### 2. Variables to Concatenate (lines 359-367)  
**Current behavior**: Only iterates over variables from the first dataset, raises KeyError if variable is missing from any other dataset
**Fix**: Also iterate over variables present in other datasets but not in the first, creating placeholder variables filled with `fill_value`

Thought: ## Complete Bug Analysis and Fix

### Root Cause
The `xarray.concat` function fails when datasets have different variables because:
1. It explicitly raises an error for "variables present in some datasets but not others" (lines 315-320)
2. It only iterates over variables from the first dataset, erroring if a variable is missing from any other dataset (lines 359-364)

However, the code already has infrastructure for handling missing values via `fill_value` (passed to `align()`), but this is only used for coordinate/index alignment, not for data variables.

### Locations That Need to Change

**File: `xarray/core/concat.py`**

#### Location 1: Lines 310-331 (variables_to_merge section)
**Current behavior:**
```python
if variables_to_merge:
    to_merge = {var: [] for var in variables_to_merge}

    for ds in datasets:
        absent_merge_vars = variables_to_merge - set(ds.variables)
        if absent_merge_vars:
            raise ValueError(
                "variables %r are present in some datasets but not others. "
                % absent_merge_vars
            )

```

**Problem:** Raises error immediately when a variable is missing from any dataset.

**Fix:** Instead of raising an error, create a placeholder variable filled with `fill_value` for each missing variable. Need to:

1. Determine the appropriate dtype (using the first available variable of the same name, or infer from fill_value)
2. Determine the appropriate dimensions 
3. Create a new variable filled with `fill_value`

#### Location 2: Lines 357-367 (stack up each variable section)
**Current behavior:**
```python
# stack up each variable to fill-out the dataset (in order)
# n.b. this loop preserves variable order, needed for groupby.
for k in datasets[0].variables:
    if k in concat_over:
        try:
            vars = ensure_common_dims([ds.variables[k] for ds in datasets])
        except KeyError:
            raise ValueError("%r is not present in all datasets." % k)
        combined = concat_vars(vars, dim, positions)
        assert isinstance(combined, Variable)
        result_vars[k] = combined
```

**Problem:** Only processes variables from the first dataset. Also doesn't handle variables that exist in later datasets but not the first.

**Fix:** 
1. Collect ALL variables from ALL datasets (not just from the first)
2. For each variable name, gather variables from all datasets, using a fill_value-loaded variable for any missing datasets
3. Also add variables that exist in later datasets but not the first

#### Additional Issue - fill_value must become numeric

When `fill_value=dtypes.NA` (the default), if there are truly missing variables, we need to infer the appropriate fill value. Looking at the test code:
```python
if fill_value == dtypes.NA:
    fill_value = np.nan
```

This conversion only happens in tests but not in the actual concat implementation. When variables are truly missing (not just missing coordinate values), we need to default to a proper fill value.

### Summary of Required Changes

1. **In `_dataset_concat` function**: Modify the variables_to_merge section to create placeholder variables with `fill_value` instead of raising errors

2. **In `_dataset_concat` function**: Modify the variable concatenation loop to find variables present in ANY dataset (not just the first) and handle missing ones with `fill_value`

3. **Default fill_value**: Consider whether the default `fill_value=dtypes.NA` should remain or if it should default to `np.nan` for better behavior with missing variables

The fix should make concat behavior consistent with `pd.concat`, which does an outer join by default and fills missing values with NaN. This is a common idiom in pandas that users expect to work similarly in xarray.

bug_report.Title: xarray.concat fails when datasets have different variables, lacks outer-join behavior like pd.concat
bug_report.Description: When attempting to concatenate xarray Datasets that contain different variables, the concat function raises ValueError exceptions indicating that variables are not present in all datasets. Users must manually create dummy variables filled with NaN or drop mismatched variables before concatenation, which is inconsistent with pandas.concat behavior.
bug_report.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption: The `_dataset_concat` function in `xarray/core/concat.py` requires all variables to be present in every dataset being concatenated. Location: lines 315-320 in the variables_to_merge section, where a ValueError is raised when `absent_merge_vars` is non-empty.
2. Incorrect assumption: The variable concatenation loop only iterates over variables from the first dataset, assuming all datasets contain those same variables. Location: lines 359-364 in `xarray/core/concat.py`, where a KeyError is raised if a variable from the first dataset is missing from any other dataset.
3. Missing implementation: The `fill_value` parameter exists and is passed to `align()` for coordinate/index alignment, but the same infrastructure is not applied to data variables that are missing from some datasets.
StepsToReproduce: Import xarray
Create two Datasets with different variables: ds1 = xr.Dataset({'a': ('x', [1, 2])}) and ds2 = xr.Dataset({'b': ('x', [3, 4])})
Attempt to concatenate: xr.concat([ds1, ds2], dim='x')
Observe ValueError about variables not present in all datasets
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Concatenation of Datasets with different variables should succeed, with missing variables filled with NaN (or the specified fill_value), similar to how pandas.concat performs an outer join by default.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: xarray.concat raises ValueError with message "variables {'variable_name'} are present in some datasets but not others" or "'variable_name' is not present in all datasets", failing to concatenate datasets that have different variables.

## Summary of Findings

### Root Cause

The bug occurs in `to_unstacked_dataset` when all variables in the original dataset have only the sample dimension(s) (i.e., only 1 dimension total that is used as the sample dimension).

**In `to_stacked_array`** (lines 3454-3468):
- When all variables have only the sample dimension(s), `stacking_dims = ()` (empty tuple)
- The stack operation at line 3468: `.stack({new_dim: (variable_dim,) + stacking_dims})` becomes `.stack({new_dim: ('variable',)})`
- This creates a MultiIndex with ONLY ONE level (just variable names like 'a', 'b')
- There are no additional levels for stacking dimensions

**In `to_unstacked_dataset`** (lines 1953-1967):
- The code assumes it can use `self.sel({variable_dim: k})` to select each variable
- With `variable_dim = 'variable'` (from the first level of the MultiIndex)
- But this approach doesn't work correctly when there's only 1 level in the MultiIndex since:
  1. After stacking, there's no separate dimension for 'variable' - it's baked into the stacked dimension 'y'
  2. The selection fails to properly restore the original coordinate structure
  3. When combining DataArrays with different coordinates into a Dataset, there's a conflict on dimension/coordinate 'y' that causes the MergeError

### The Bug Location

**Primary fix location**: `xarray/core/dataarray.py`, method `to_unstacked_dataset` (lines 1901-1967)

The current code:
```python
# pull variables out of datarray
data_dict = {}
for k in variables:
    data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

# unstacked dataset
return Dataset(data_dict)
```

This approach fails when:
- `variables` come from a single-level MultiIndex (i.e., stacking_dims was empty)
- The `.sel()` operation doesn't properly handle this case
- The resulting DataArrays have coordinates that conflict when passed to Dataset()

### Additional Issue

There's also a related issue in `to_stacked_array` at lines 3478-3483:
```python
idx = data_array.indexes[new_dim]
levels = [idx.levels[0]] + [
    level.astype(self[level.name].dtype) for level in idx.levels[1:]
]
```

When there's only 1 level, `idx.levels[1:]` is empty, and this code works. But the real issue is in the `to_unstacked_dataset` which doesn't properly reverse this operation when there's only 1 level.

### Complete Fix

The fix needs to handle the case when the MultiIndex has only 1 level (meaning `stacking_dims` was empty during stacking). There are several approaches:

1. **Use `.unstack()` method** in `to_unstacked_dataset` when appropriate
2. **Check for single-level MultiIndex** and handle it differently

Here's my recommended fix. The key change is in `to_unstacked_dataset`:

```python
# Current code (broken):
idx = self.indexes[dim]
if not isinstance(idx, pd.MultiIndex):
    raise ValueError(f"'{dim}' is not a stacked coordinate")

level_number = idx._get_level_number(level)
variables = idx.levels[level_number]
variable_dim = idx.names[level_number]

# pull variables out of datarray
data_dict = {}
for k in variables:
    data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

# unstacked dataset
return Dataset(data_dict)
```

**The fix** should:
1. Check if the MultiIndex has only 1 level (the variable level)
2. If so, use `.unstack(dim)` to properly expand the dimensions
3. Then construct the Dataset properly from the unstacked result

The key insight is that when there was only 1 stacking level (i.e., all variables had the same dimensions as sample_dims), we need to use `.unstack()` which properly reverses the `.stack()` operation, rather than trying to select by the variable level.

bug_report.Title: to_unstacked_dataset broken for single-dim variables
bug_report.Description: Calling to_unstacked_dataset on a DataArray that was created via to_stacked_array fails with a MergeError when the original variables have only a single dimension (i.e., the sample dimension). The roundtrip from Dataset to stacked array and back fails for single-dimension variables.
RootCause: 1. In to_stacked_array: when all variables have only the sample dimension(s), the stacking_dims becomes an empty tuple, causing the stack operation to create a MultiIndex with only ONE level (the variable names), without additional levels for stacking dimensions.

2. In to_unstacked_dataset (xarray/core/dataarray.py, method to_unstacked_dataset, lines ~1901-1967): the code attempts to use self.sel({variable_dim: k}) to select each variable, assuming a separate 'variable' dimension exists. This assumption is incorrect when the MultiIndex has only 1 level — the 'variable' level is baked into the stacked dimension 'y' and there is no separate dimension for variable_dim.

3. The .sel() operation fails to properly restore the original coordinate structure, resulting in DataArrays with coordinates that conflict when passed to the Dataset constructor, causing the MergeError: 'conflicting values for variable y on objects to be combined'.
StepsToReproduce: Create a DataArray with a single dimension 'x': arr = xr.DataArray(np.arange(3), coords=[('x', [0, 1, 2])])
Create a Dataset with variables 'a' and 'b' using the same array: data = xr.Dataset({'a': arr, 'b': arr})
Stack the dataset: stacked = data.to_stacked_array('y', sample_dims=['x'])
Attempt to unstack: unstacked = stacked.to_unstacked_dataset('y')
Observe MergeError
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: A successful roundtrip — to_unstacked_dataset should reconstruct the original Dataset with variables 'a' and 'b', each with dimension 'x', without errors.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: A MergeError is raised: 'conflicting values for variable y on objects to be combined. You can skip this check by specifying compat="override".'

## Investigation Findings

### What the feature request is about

This is a feature request to display the units (stored in `attrs["units"]`) in the xarray Dataset repr for both coordinates and data variables.

**Example of desired output:**
```
<xarray.Dataset>
Dimensions:  (time: 3, x: 988, y: 822)
Coordinates:
  * x, in metres         (x)            float64 ...
  * y, in metres         (y)            float64 ...
  * time                 (time)         datetime64[ns] ...
Data variables:
    rainfall, in mm      (time, y, x)   float32 ...
    max_temp, in deg C   (time, y, x)   float32 ...
```

### Root Cause

The current code in `xarray/core/formatting.py` doesn't read the "units" attribute from variables when generating the repr. Units are commonly stored in `variable.attrs["units"]` per CF conventions and are used throughout xarray and other scientific data tools.

### Locations that Need Modification

**1. `summarize_variable()` in `xarray/core/formatting.py` (lines 271-291)**

This is the core function that generates each line in the dataset repr for both coordinates and data variables. Currently it has:
```python

    name: Hashable, var, col_width: int, marker: str = " ", max_width: int = None
):
    """Summarize a variable in one line, e.g., for the Dataset.__repr__."""
    # ... 
    first_col = pretty_print(f"  {marker} {name} ", col_width)
    if var.dims:
        dims_str = "({}) ".format(", ".join(map(str, var.dims)))
    else:
        dims_str = ""
    front_str = f"{first_col}{dims_str}{var.dtype} "
    # ...
```

**Change needed**: Add code to check for `attrs.get("units")` and include it in the formatted output:

```python

    name: Hashable, var, col_width: int, marker: str = " ", max_width: int = None
):
    """Summarize a variable in one line, e.g., for the Dataset.__repr__."""
    # ... existing code ...
    
    # Get units from attrs if available
    units = getattr(var, 'attrs', {}).get('units')
    if units:
        name_str = f"{name}, in {units}"
    else:
        name_str = str(name)
    
    first_col = pretty_print(f"  {marker} {name_str} ", col_width)
    # ... rest of the code
```

**2. `_get_col_items()` in `xarray/core/formatting.py` (lines 342-356)**

This function computes the column width for alignment. Currently it only considers the variable name:

```python

    """Get all column items to format, including both keys of `mapping`
    and MultiIndex levels if any.
    """

    col_items = []
    for k, v in mapping.items():
        col_items.append(k)
        # ... rest
```

**Change needed**: Need to include units in the column width calculation:
```python
def _get_col_items(mapping):
    """Get all column items to format, including both keys of `mapping`
    and MultiIndex levels if any.
    """
    from .variable import IndexVariable

    col_items = []
    for k, v in mapping.items():
        var = getattr(v, "variable", v)
        units = getattr(var, 'attrs', {}).get('units')
        if units:
            col_items.append(f"{k}, in {units}")
        else:
            col_items.append(k)
        # ... rest
```

**Note**: The `summarize_coord()` function (lines 312-324) and `summarize_datavar()` function (lines 308-309) both pass `var.variable` to `summarize_variable()`. The Variable object has the `.attrs` attribute, so the units should be accessible without additional changes to those functions.

### Implementation Details

- The units string format in the request is ", in [units]" - e.g., ", in metres" or ", in mm"
- If no units attribute exists, the output should remain unchanged (no units shown)
- The column width calculation (`_get_col_items` and `_calculate_col_width`) must be updated to ensure proper alignment when some variables have units and others don't

### Related files that might also need updates

1. **`xarray/core/formatting_html.py`** - the HTML representation might also need similar changes for consistency in Jupyter notebooks
2. **Tests** - any tests that check the exact output of the repr would need updates

The core fix is in `xarray/core/formatting.py` in the functions mentioned above.

bug_report.Description: The xarray Dataset repr does not display units for coordinates and data variables, even when units are stored in the variable's attrs dictionary per CF conventions. Users must manually inspect each variable's attrs to determine units, which reduces the utility of the repr for understanding dataset contents at a glance.
bug_report.RootCause:
1. The `summarize_variable()` function in `xarray/core/formatting.py` (lines 271-291) generates the repr line for each variable but does not read or display the 'units' attribute from `var.attrs['units']`.

bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When a variable has a 'units' attribute defined in its attrs dictionary, the Dataset repr should display it in the format '{name}, in {units}' (e.g., 'rainfall, in mm', 'x, in metres'). Variables without units should display normally without modification.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: The Dataset repr shows variable names without any unit information, even when the variable's attrs dictionary contains a 'units' key. For example, a variable with attrs={'units': 'mm'} displays simply as 'rainfall' rather than 'rainfall, in mm'.

    and consists of variables, coordinates and attributes which
    together form a self describing dataset.

    Dataset implements the mapping interface with keys given by variable
    names and values given by DataArray objects for each variable name.

    One dimensional variables with name equal to their dimension are

    To load data from a file or file-like object, use the `open_dataset`
    function.

    Parameters

        the form ``(dims, data[, attrs])`` which can be used as
        arguments to create a new ``Variable``. Each dimension must
        have the same length in all variables in which it appears.

        The following notations are accepted:

        - mapping {var name: DataArray}
        - mapping {var name: Variable}
        - mapping {var name: (dimension name, array-like)}
        - mapping {var name: (tuple of dimension names, array-like)}
        - mapping {dimension name: array-like}
          (it will be automatically moved to coords, see below)

        Each dimension must have the same length in all variables in
        which it appears.
    coords : dict-like, optional
        Another mapping in similar form as the `data_vars` argument,
        except the each item is saved on the dataset as a "coordinate".
        These variables have an associated meaning: they describe
        constant/fixed/independent quantities, unlike the
        varying/measured/dependent quantities that belong in
        `variables`. Coordinates values may be given by 1-dimensional
        arrays or scalars, in which case `dims` do not need to be
        supplied: 1D arrays will be assumed to give index values along
        the dimension with the same name.

        The following notations are accepted:

        - mapping {coord name: DataArray}
        - mapping {coord name: Variable}
        - mapping {coord name: (dimension name, array-like)}
        - mapping {coord name: (tuple of dimension names, array-like)}
        - mapping {dimension name: array-like}

          coord name)

        The last notation implies that the coord name is the same as
        the dimension name.

bug_report.Title: DataSet.update and Dataset constructor with tuple format cause dask arrays to compute eagerly
bug_report.Description: When using DataSet.update with a dictionary containing a tuple of (dims, DataArray), or when creating a Dataset with a tuple in the format ('dim', DataArray), chunked dask arrays lose their chunking and are computed eagerly. The second issue specifically occurs when passing a DataArray (rather than a raw dask array) as the second element of a tuple when constructing a Dataset.
bug_report.RootCause:
1. The Dataset.update method calls as_compatible_data() on the values being inserted. When the value is a DataArray (as in tuple[1] = DataArray), as_compatible_data invokes the DataArray's data property, which triggers computation of dask arrays.

2. In the Dataset constructor, when processing entries in the format dict(a=(('x', 'y'), DataArray)), the code extracts the DataArray from tuple position [1] and passes it to as_compatible_data(). The DataArray.data property getter is accessed, which for a DataArray backed by a dask array returns the computed numpy array instead of preserving the dask array.

3. The DataArray.data property is implemented as: return self.variable.data, which for a DataArray wrapping a dask array ends up triggering computation because Variable.data getter accesses _unary_op or similar paths that materialize the array.
StepsToReproduce: Import xarray and numpy: import xarray as xr; import numpy as np
Create a chunked DataArray: foo = xr.DataArray(np.random.randn(3, 3), dims=('x', 'y')).chunk()
Create a Dataset with the chunkedDataArray: ds = xr.Dataset({'foo': foo, 'bar': ('x', [1, 2, 3])})
Verify foo is still chunked: print(ds.foo.data)
Create update dict with tuple format: update_dict = {'foo': (('x', 'y'), ds.foo[1:, :]), 'bar': ('x', ds.bar[1:])}
Verify the DataArray in tuple is still chunked: print(update_dict['foo'][1].data)
Call ds.update(update_dict)
Observe foo is now a numpy array: print(type(ds.foo.data))  # should be numpy.ndarray, not dask.array.Array
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: The chunked dask DataArray should remain chunked after calling DataSet.update. The dask array should not be computed eagerly.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: After DataSet.update, the previously chunked DataArray is converted to a numpy array, losing its lazy evaluation capability. Similarly, creating a Dataset with dict(a=(('x', 'y'), DataArray)) where DataArray wraps a dask array triggers immediate computation.

class DataArrayResample(DataArrayGroupBy, Resample):
    """DataArrayGroupBy object specialized to time resampling operations over a
    specified dimension
    """

    def __init__(self, *args, dim=None, resample_dim=None, **kwargs):

        if dim == resample_dim:
            raise ValueError(
                "Proxy resampling dimension ('{}') "
                "cannot have the same name as actual dimension "
                "('{}')! ".format(resample_dim, dim)
            )
        self._dim = dim
        self._resample_dim = resample_dim

        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def map(self, func, shortcut=False, args=(), **kwargs):
        """Apply a function to each array in the group and concatenate them
        together into a new array.

        `func` is called like `func(ar, *args, **kwargs)` for each array `ar`
        in this group.

        Apply uses heuristics (like `pandas.GroupBy.apply`) to figure out how
        to stack together the array. The rule is:

        1. If the dimension along which the group coordinate is defined is
           still in the first grouped array after applying `func`, then stack
           over this dimension.
        2. Otherwise, stack over the new dimension given by name of this
           grouping (the argument to the `groupby` function).

        Parameters
        ----------
        func : callable
            Callable to apply to each array.
        shortcut : bool, optional
            Whether or not to shortcut evaluation under the assumptions that:

            (1) The action of `func` does not depend on any of the array
                metadata (attributes or coordinates) but only on the data and
                dimensions.
            (2) The action of `func` creates arrays with homogeneous metadata,
                that is, with the same dimensions and attributes.

            If these conditions are satisfied `shortcut` provides significant
            speedup. This should be the case for many common groupby operations
            (e.g., applying numpy ufuncs).
        args : tuple, optional
            Positional arguments passed on to `func`.
        **kwargs
            Used to call `func(ar, **kwargs)` for each array `ar`.

        Returns
        -------
        applied : DataArray or DataArray
            The result of splitting, applying and combining this array.
        """
        # TODO: the argument order for Resample doesn't match that for its parent,
        # GroupBy
        combined = super().map(func, shortcut=shortcut, args=args, **kwargs)

        # If the aggregation function didn't drop the original resampling
        # dimension, then we need to do so before we can rename the proxy
        # dimension we used.
        if self._dim in combined.coords:
            combined = combined.drop_vars(self._dim)

        if self._resample_dim in combined.dims:
            combined = combined.rename({self._resample_dim: self._dim})

        return combined

    def apply(self, func, args=(), shortcut=None, **kwargs):
        """
        Backward compatible implementation of ``map``

        See Also
        --------
        DataArrayResample.map
        """
        warnings.warn(
            "Resample.apply may be deprecated in the future. Using Resample.map is encouraged",
            PendingDeprecationWarning,
            stacklevel=2,
        )
        return self.map(func=func, shortcut=shortcut, args=args, **kwargs)

Thought: ## Investigation Summary

I've identified the root cause of the trailing whitespace issue in the `DatasetGroupBy` text representation.

### Root Cause

The bug is in the `__repr__` method of the `GroupBy` base class in `xarray/core/groupby.py` at line 439:

```python
def __repr__(self):
    return "{}, grouped over {!r} \n{!r} groups with labels {}.".format(
        self.__class__.__name__,
        self._unique_coord.name,
        self._unique_coord.size,
        ", ".join(format_array_flat(self._unique_coord, 30).split()),
    )
```

The format string on line 439 contains `' \n'` (a space followed by a newline) instead of just `'\n'`. This creates the trailing whitespace before the newline.

### Locations That Need to Be Fixed

**Primary fix - `xarray/core/groupby.py` line 439:**
- **Current code:** `"{}, grouped over {!r} \n{!r} groups with labels {}."`
- **Fix:** Remove the space before `\n` → `"{}, grouped over {!r}\n{!r} groups with labels {}."`

This fix in the `GroupBy` base class will automatically fix `DatasetGroupBy`, `DataArrayGroupBy`, and their resampling variants (`DatasetResample`, `DataArrayResample`) since they all inherit from `GroupBy`.

**Additional issues found - `xarray/core/resample.py`:**

There are also trailing whitespaces in error message format strings:

1. **Lines 170-172** - The error message when `dim == resample_dim`:
   ```python
   "Proxy resampling dimension ('{}') "
   "cannot have the same name as actual dimension "
   "('{}')! ".format(resample_dim, dim)
   ```
   The second line `"('{}')! "` has a trailing space.

2. **Lines 262-264** - Same issue in the `DatasetResample` class:


### Why This Happens

Looking at the format string construction, it appears someone may have intended to add spacing for visual formatting in the output but inadvertently left a trailing space that shows up in the string representation.

```python
>>> str(ds.groupby("letters"))
"DatasetGroupBy, grouped over 'letters'\n2 groups with labels 'a', 'b'."
```

Instead of the current:
```python
"DatasetGroupBy, grouped over 'letters' \n2 groups with labels 'a', 'b'."
```

bug_report.Title: Trailing whitespace in DatasetGroupBy text representation
bug_report.Description: The string representation of DatasetGroupBy (and related GroupBy classes) contains a trailing space before the newline character in the first line of output.
bug_report.RootCause: 1. The __repr__ method of the GroupBy base class in xarray/core/groupby.py at line 439 uses the format string '{}, grouped over {!r} \n{!r} groups with labels {}.' which contains a space between {!r} and \n, creating trailing whitespace in the output. 2. In xarray/core/resample.py lines 170-172, the error message format string '({})! ' contains a trailing space after the exclamation mark. 3. In xarray/core/resample.py lines 262-264, the same trailing space issue exists in the DatasetResample class error message.
StepsToReproduce: Import xarray and numpy: import xarray as xr, numpy as np
Create a dataset: ds = xr.Dataset({'foo': (('x', 'y'), np.random.rand(4, 3))}, coords={'x': [10, 20, 30, 40], 'letters': ('x', list('abba'))})
Call str(ds.groupby('letters'))
Observe the trailing whitespace before the newline in the output
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: The string representation should display exactly 'DatasetGroupBy, grouped over 'letters'\n2 groups with labels 'a', 'b'.' without any trailing whitespace before the newline character.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: The string representation contains a trailing space in the first line: 'DatasetGroupBy, grouped over 'letters' \n2 groups with labels 'a', 'b'.' where there is a space after 'letters' and before \n.
